CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (Singlish UD treebank, POS and dependency models with neural stacking, and empirical gains) are stated early and revisited in experiments and conclusion. Notation and methodology are consistently presented and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (scores by phenomenon, ablations) are informative. A few minor presentation issues remain: the writeup could more clearly explain the UD v2 guideline limitations and how they affected annotation choices, and some examples of challenging Singlish phenomena would benefit from tighter linking to the formal analysis. From a theory-purist perspective the paper communicates its work clearly but could strengthen the linguistic exposition connecting analyses to UD decisions. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusion; the main contributions (new 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, neural stacking transfer methods, and empirical gains) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (e.g. per-phenomenon analyses and embedding comparisons) are informative for practitioners. Writing quality is good with few grammatical issues, though a couple minor clarity points remain (e.g. precise availability/timing of UD resources and some implementation details) that prevent it from being exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (Singlish UD treebank, POS and dependency models with neural stacking, and empirical gains) are stated early and revisited in experiments and conclusion. Notation and methodology are consistently presented and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (scores by phenomenon, ablations) are informative. A few minor presentation issues remain: the writeup could more clearly explain the UD v2 guideline limitations and how they affected annotation choices, and some examples of challenging Singlish phenomena would benefit from tighter linking to the formal analysis. From a theory-purist perspective the paper communicates its work clearly but could strengthen the linguistic exposition connecting analyses to UD decisions.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from motivation through methods, experiments, and conclusion; the main contributions (new 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, neural stacking transfer methods, and empirical gains) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (e.g. per-phenomenon analyses and embedding comparisons) are informative for practitioners. Writing quality is good with few grammatical issues, though a couple minor clarity points remain (e.g. precise availability/timing of UD resources and some implementation details) that prevent it from being exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper’s main contribution is empirical—the creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank—and that the modeling relies on existing methods (biaffine parser, bi-LSTM-CRF tagger, and neural stacking) rather than novel algorithms. Given the value of the new low-resource dataset and the pragmatic, non-trivial application that yields measurable gains, but the lack of methodological innovation, a middle score of 3 appropriately reflects moderate originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper primarily applies existing architectures (Dozat & Manning biaffine parser, bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger) and a known feature-level neural stacking transfer technique to a new low-resource variety, rather than introducing a new algorithmic or theoretical advance. The main contribution is a small Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences), which I must penalize as a dataset contribution below my 5k threshold for substantive novelty. While the empirical gains and use of Singlish-specific embeddings are useful engineering work, they do not constitute a non-trivial methodological extension that would surprise an expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main novelty is empirical: assembling and releasing a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, which is a meaningful new resource for a low-resource, typologically distinct lect — I value that contribution highly. The modeling side applies existing components (Dozat & Manning biaffine parser, bi-LSTM-CRF tagger) and the feature-level neural stacking transfer technique to this new dataset; this is a pragmatic, non-trivial application but not a fundamentally new method. The authors do clearly separate resource creation from the transfer technique and demonstrate measurable gains using Singlish embeddings and neural stacking, so the work is a useful, moderate-novel contribution rather than a breakthrough in methodology.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by sensible baselines and methodology, and that the phenomenon-level analyses strengthen the evidence. The main weaknesses are the small (1,200-sentence) treebank and the absence of reported statistical significance and finer-grained ablations, which limit confidence in generality but do not fundamentally undermine the results; for these reasons I assign a slightly positive score of 4/5.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claims (UAS/LAS/POS gains from neural stacking and Singlish embeddings) are stated and supported by experiments showing substantial relative error reductions, and standard architectures and baselines (Singlish-only, English-on-Singlish) are used. However, from the summary I see no mention of statistical significance testing or rigorous ablations isolating the contribution of each component (stacking vs. embeddings vs. data size), and the core resource is very small (1,200 sentences) which by my standards should be penalised and raises questions about robustness. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and while the conclusions are plausible given results, the limited data and missing significance/ablation analyses constitute nontrivial concerns about the technical completeness of the validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: a new 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, Singlish-specific embeddings, and experiments showing substantial UAS/LAS and POS accuracy gains from feature-level neural stacking versus Singlish-only and English-transfer baselines. The methodology is sound (Dozat & Manning parser, bi-LSTM-CRF tagger, sensible baselines, and reported hyperparameters) and the phenomenon-level analyses strengthen the evidence. Minor technical concerns are the lack of reported statistical significance testing and the small size of the Singlish treebank (acknowledged), which slightly limit confidence in generality but do not undermine the main conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments are solid, reproducible, and the paper includes useful baselines, embedding comparisons, and phenomenon-level analyses; code, data, and hyperparameters are provided. I award a 4 because the empirical evaluation is thorough and practical, but I acknowledge the small 1,200‑sentence treebank and the lack of more extensive ablation/sensitivity and statistical testing, which limit stronger theoretical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents reasonable experiments (POS and dependency parsing with neural stacking, comparisons to English-on-Singlish and embeddings variants, and phenomenon-level analyses) and provides code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. However, the Singlish treebank is very small (1,200 sentences), which under my standards for theoretical claims is a serious limitation and warrants penalty. The work reports gains from neural stacking but lacks the rigorous ablation/sensitivity analyses and statistical significance testing I require to attribute improvements to specific components. Related work is adequately covered and some limitations are acknowledged, but the experimental depth is only adequate rather than thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid: a new 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, strong baselines (Singlish-only, English-on-Singlish), comparisons of embeddings (ICE-SIN vs English) and neural stacking for POS and dependency, plus phenomenon-level analyses that support the claims. Ablations are present in the form of baseline vs neural-stacked models and embedding variants, though more sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying amounts of Singlish data or alternative transfer strategies) would strengthen the claims. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, limitations and dataset/code release are acknowledged, and hyperparameters are provided, so reproduction is feasible. Overall this is a thorough, practical evaluation with only minor gaps in additional ablation/sensitivity studies.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is well within the venue scope, uses appropriate methods and evaluations, and contributes a new Singlish UD treebank with useful phenomenon-level analyses. Reviewer A correctly notes the limited dataset size and lack of major algorithmic novelty, while Reviewer B emphasizes the value of the dataset, reproducibility, and empirical gains from transfer. Given these points, a middle-ground score reflects the paper's clear practical value and fit despite modest novelty and annotation scale.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue (dependency parsing, low-resource/cross-lingual transfer) and uses expected methods and evaluations (UAS/LAS, neural stacking, Dozat & Manning parser). However, its main contributions are a small Singlish treebank (1,200 sentences—well below my 5k threshold) and the application of existing neural-stacking transfer techniques rather than new theoretical or algorithmic advances. The empirical evaluation and analyses are appropriate, but given the limited annotation size and lack of clear theoretical novelty, the fit is borderline rather than a strong match for a top-tier conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it addresses dependency parsing and low-resource transfer — core topics for ACL/EMNLP — and contributes a new Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) plus reproducible code and embeddings experiments. The methodology (biaffine parser, bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger, and feature-level neural stacking from English) and standard evaluation metrics (UAS/LAS/POS accuracy) meet community expectations, and the phenomenon-level analyses (copula deletion, topic prominence, discourse particles) are especially valuable for applied and resource-focused audiences. Given the dataset creation, solid empirical gains from cross-lingual transfer, and public release, the work clearly targets and serves the conference readership.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against sensible baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, embedding variants) using standard metrics and ablations, so the evaluation is largely fair. However, important modern and cross‑lingual baselines (multilingual pretrained models, delexicalized/annotation‑projection methods) are missing and the very small treebank limits claims of robustness, so the comparative evaluation is incomplete — hence a middle‑low score of 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does compare against reasonable baselines (Singlish-only parser/POS, and English-on-Singlish) using the same UD metrics and datasets (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy), and reports clear gains from neural stacking and ICE‑SIN embeddings. However, given the prominence of stronger cross‑lingual and pretrained approaches in recent work (e.g. multilingual contextual models or alternative transfer/adaptation methods), those competitive baselines are not reported, and the very small treebank (1,200 sentences) further raises concerns about whether the comparisons demonstrate robust superiority. From a theory‑purist perspective this is therefore a useful but incomplete evaluation of comparative effectiveness.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against sensible and strong baselines: a Singlish-only parser/tagger, an English parser applied to Singlish (ENG-on-SIN), and variants using different embeddings (ICE-SIN, GloVe, Giga). Comparisons use the same Singlish treebank and standard metrics (UAS/LAS/POS accuracy) and include ablations (neural stacking vs. Singlish-only), which makes the setup largely fair. However, it lacks comparison to other practical transfer approaches (e.g., delexicalized transfer, annotation projection, or multilingual pretrained models) that would be relevant for low-resource cross-lingual transfer, so some important baselines are missing.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful, reproducible contribution by releasing a Singlish UD treebank, code, and demonstrating effective transfer techniques with substantial empirical gains, but is not transformative for general parsing theory. The main limitation is the small size of the annotated resource and limited algorithmic novelty, so the work has meaningful practical impact for low-resource and creole/colloquial parsing but only moderate broader impact—hence a mid-level score.

reviewer_a.justification: While creating a Singlish UD treebank and demonstrating cross-lingual neural stacking for POS and parsing is a useful engineering contribution, the paper’s impact is limited by the very small annotated resource (1,200 sentences) — well below my threshold of 5k — and by the lack of theoretical or algorithmic novelty beyond applying existing models (Dozat & Manning biaffine parser, bi-LSTM-CRF) for transfer. The results show practical gains (UAS 84.21%, substantial relative error reduction) and the release of code/data may help others working on creole/colloquial varieties, but the work is primarily an incremental, resource-oriented advance rather than something likely to reshape research directions or broader theory.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem—dependency parsing for a low-resource, typologically distinct English variety—by creating a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and releasing code, which is valuable for the community. The empirical results show substantial, reproducible gains from feature-level neural stacking and Singlish-specific embeddings (e.g., UAS 84.21%, large relative error reductions), demonstrating a practical and effective transfer technique that others can apply. The phenomenon-level analyses (topic-prominence, copula deletion, NP deletion, discourse particles) and use of public resources make it likely to be built upon by researchers working on low-resource and creole/colloquial varieties. While not transformative for general parsing theory, it meaningfully advances resources and methods for low-resource syntactic parsing.

main_claims: Created a Singlish dependency treebank of 1,200 sentences annotated under the Universal Dependencies scheme.

Trained a Singlish dependency parser based on the Dozat & Manning (2016) biaffine attention model and improved it via feature-level neural stacking to transfer English syntactic knowledge, achieving substantial gains (reported UAS 84.21%, ~36.51% relative error reduction over baseline).
Trained a Singlish POS tagger using a bi-LSTM-CRF with neural stacking from an English POS model, improving tagging accuracy to 84.79% (31.58% relative error reduction over Singlish-only baseline).

Showed that using Singlish-specific embeddings (ICE-SIN) and neural stacking yields better performance than using English embeddings alone, and provided analyses by Singlish grammatical phenomena (topic-prominence, copula deletion, NP deletion, discourse particles).
_summary.methodology: Train a base POS tagger (bi-LSTM-CRF) and base dependency parser (Dozat & Manning biaffine parser) on English UD data, then apply feature-level neural stacking to integrate English model feature layers into Singlish POS and dependency models, using Singlish treebank data and Singlish embeddings for fine-tuning.
datasets: ICE-SIN (Singapore Component of the International Corpus of English)
SENNA embeddings (50-dimension, used for POS base model)
baselines: Baseline Singlish parser trained on Singlish treebank (Base-ICE-SIN)
Singlish POS tagger trained only on Singlish treebank
limitations: Limited amount of Singlish training data led to low tagging accuracy for the Singlish-only POS model.
Only guidelines for Universal Dependencies v2 were available but not the English corpus when the work was completed (stated availability issue).
Parser differences versus prior work are partially due to not using fine-grained POS tags.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dependency parsing / low-resource / cross-lingual transfer

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a decade of experience in syntactic theory and formal grammar; faculty member who reviews for ACL and TACL.
traits: penalises papers whose main contribution is dataset construction under 5k annotated sentences (will deduct points if < 5k)
requires clear theoretical or algorithmic novelty beyond application of existing models (will mark down if the method is mainly engineering/transfer of known architectures)
demands rigorous ablation studies and statistical significance testing for claimed gains (will deduct if no ablations showing what components provide gains or no significance)
prefers linguistic analysis that links findings to formal syntactic/typological theory rather than only error statistics (will downgrade purely empirical error breakdowns)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritises principled, theory-driven contributions and penalises papers that are primarily empirical or incremental model-transfer work without new algorithms or strong theoretical insight.

reviewer_b.name: Resource & Application Champion
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher focused on low-resource languages, cross-lingual transfer, and dataset curation; regular reviewer for EMNLP and CoNLL and organiser of shared tasks.
traits: rewards creation of new, well-documented language resources even if modest in size (gives positive credit for < 5k but > 500 annotated sentences)
favors practical transfer techniques that yield measurable improvements in low-resource settings (scores higher when empirical gains are substantial and reproducible)
values thorough, task-focused analyses and baseline comparisons (penalises when the paper lacks comparisons to straightforward strong baselines, but rewards comprehensive phenomenon-level analysis)
prefers methods that are readily usable by the community (gives extra credit for releasing code/embeddings/treebank or using publicly available pre-trained models)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Supports empirical, resource-driven work that advances capabilities for low-resource languages and prioritises practical effectiveness, reproducibility, and useful linguistic analyses over purely theoretical novelty.
